sections.title: Phrasal verbs
items: **bring up** — take care of a child until he or she becomes an adult
**fall out (with)** — have an argument with sb and stop being friends
**go out with** — be the boyfriend/girlfriend of

exercises.title: Topic vocabulary (gap fill)
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with words from the box.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with words from the box.

items.sentence: You should ...... to Max for forgetting his birthday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is you should say sorry, so the correct answer is "apologise".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: Don't open the door to a ...... when you're home alone.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is someone unknown, so the correct answer is "stranger".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: They live in a busy ...... near the station.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is area where people live, so the correct answer is "neighbourhood".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: My uncle is very ...... and often pays for everyone's meal.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is pays for everyone, so the correct answer is "generous".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: I'm really ...... to my neighbours for helping us move house.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is thankful for help, so the correct answer is "grateful".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: She's in a cheerful ...... because her best friend is visiting.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is cheerful feeling, so the correct answer is "mood".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: You should always ...... other people's private opinions.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is value other opinions, so the correct answer is "respect".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We're very ...... friends and we tell each other everything.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is tell each other everything, so the correct answer is "close".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

exercises.title: Circle the correct word
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.originalSentence: Jack wants to be **independent / ordinary** and pay his own rent.
items.completedSentence: Jack wants to be independent and pay his own rent.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "independent" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "independent" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "ordinary" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.ordinary: "ordinary" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("and pay his own rent.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: their flat before the guests arrived.
items.originalSentence: They decided to **decorate / recognise** their flat before the guests arrived.
items.completedSentence: They decided to decorate their flat before the guests arrived.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "decorate" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "decorate" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "recognise" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.recognise: "recognise" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("their flat before the guests arrived.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: I can always **trust / rent** my cousin with a secret.
items.completedSentence: I can always trust my cousin with a secret.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "trust" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "trust" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "rent" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.rent: "rent" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("my cousin with a secret.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Can you **introduce / defend** me to your new neighbour?
items.completedSentence: Can you introduce me to your new neighbour?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "introduce" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "introduce" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "defend" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.defend: "defend" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("me to your new neighbour?").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: before, but Dad says he's my uncle.
items.originalSentence: I've never met that **relation / guest** before, but Dad says he's my uncle.
items.completedSentence: I've never met that relation before, but Dad says he's my uncle.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "relation" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "relation" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "guest" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.guest: "guest" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("before, but Dad says he's my uncle.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: , but they are still good friends.
items.originalSentence: My parents are **divorced / private**, but they are still good friends.
items.completedSentence: My parents are divorced , but they are still good friends.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "divorced" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "divorced" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "private" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.private: "private" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (", but they are still good friends.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: Phrasal verbs (complete the gaps)
exercises.instructions: Write the correct form of the phrasal verb in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write the correct form of the phrasal verb in each gap.

items.sentence: My parents worked hard to ...... three children.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is raise children, so the correct answer is "bring up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "bring up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: I don't want to ...... Emma over a silly argument.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is have an argument, so the correct answer is "fall out with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "fall out with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Do you ...... your brother well?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is have a good relationship, so the correct answer is "get on with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "get on with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Zoe used to ...... Daniel, but they aren't together now.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is be in a romantic relationship, so the correct answer is "go out with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "go out with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: I promised to help, so I don't want to ...... anyone.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is disappoint someone, so the correct answer is "let down".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "let down", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Can you ...... the dog while we're away?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is take care of, so the correct answer is "look after".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "look after", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: Prepositional phrases (matching)
exercises.instructions: Match sentence beginnings with the correct ending.
exercises.studentInstruction: Match sentence beginnings with the correct ending.

items.sentence: She cooked the whole meal by ...... .
My best friend and I have a lot in ...... .
I'm still in ...... with some friends from my old school.
They are completely in ...... with each other.
items.explanation: Romantic phrase → in **love** with each other.

items.sentence: I didn't hurt your feelings on ...... .
He lives on his ...... in a small flat.

exercises.title: Word patterns (adjectives, verbs and nouns)
exercises.instructions: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).
exercises.studentInstruction: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).

items.sentence: I'm very fond **D** of my grandparents.
items.explanation: **Fond of** is the correct pattern — we feel fond **of** grandparents.

items.sentence: Leo is jealous **A** of his sister's success.
Please be kind **F** to the new student.
My aunt is married **B** to a musician.
We're proud **E** of our cousin's achievements.
She apologised **C** for being late.
They argued **G** about money again.
I admire him **H** for his honesty.

exercises.title: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: I have great ...... for people who always help others. (ADMIRE)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from ADMIRE, so the correct answer is "admiration".

wrongOptions.admire: The root ADMIRE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "admiration".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ADMIRE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ADMIRE → admiration: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "admiration" is the word-formation answer from the root ADMIRE in this context.

items.sentence: It took a long time, but she finally offered him ...... . (FORGIVE)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from FORGIVE, so the correct answer is "forgiveness".

wrongOptions.forgive: The root FORGIVE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "forgiveness".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of FORGIVE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: FORGIVE → forgiveness: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "forgiveness" is the word-formation answer from the root FORGIVE in this context.

items.sentence: I value ...... more than anything in a friendship. (HONEST)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from HONEST, so the correct answer is "honesty".

wrongOptions.honest: The root HONEST cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "honesty".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of HONEST that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: HONEST → honesty: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "honesty" is the word-formation answer from the root HONEST in this context.

items.sentence: The teacher made a short ...... before the new student spoke. (INTRODUCE)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from INTRODUCE, so the correct answer is "introduction".

wrongOptions.introduce: The root INTRODUCE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "introduction".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of INTRODUCE that fits grammatically, not just the root.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "introduction" is the word-formation answer from the root INTRODUCE in this context.

items.sentence: Moving away from home gave him a lot of ...... . (CONFIDENT)

wrongOptions.confident: The root CONFIDENT cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "confidence".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of CONFIDENT that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: CONFIDENT → confidence: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "confidence" is the word-formation answer from the root CONFIDENT in this context.

items.sentence: Nobody believed him because everyone knew he was a ...... . (LIE)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is person who lies, so the correct answer is "liar".

wrongOptions.lie: The root LIE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "liar".

wrongOptions.able: The root ABLE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "ability".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ABLE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.

exercises.title: Mixed revision
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with one suitable word.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with one suitable word.

items.sentence: I don't want to argue ...... my parents tonight.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Argue with someone (not argue about parents here).
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: They ...... up after a long relationship.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is end a relationship, so the correct answer is "split".

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
